codeReview.summary: Clean, thorough removal of subpixel (LCD) rendering from the full glyph pipeline. All four changed files (rasterize.rs, cache.rs, drawing/lib.rs, test/tests/drawing.rs) are consistent in switching from 3-byte RGB coverage to 1-byte grayscale coverage. The rasterizer correctly eliminates OVERSAMPLE_X=6, the FIR color-fringe filter, and the GREYSCALE_AA averaging path. Vertical oversampling (OVERSAMPLE_Y=8) is correctly preserved. draw_coverage correctly uses a single alpha value uniformly for R, G, B channels with sRGB gamma-correct blending. All unsafe blocks retain their SAFETY comments. No dead code remains — OVERSAMPLE_X, FIR filter, and subpixel downsample logic are fully deleted. GlyphCache size correctly reduced from ~1.3MB to ~238KB. Tests comprehensively cover the new format: rasterizer output length, cache formats, draw_coverage uniform blending, stem darkening, and all-ASCII rasterization. QEMU visual verification performed. 1,782 tests pass.

issues.description: STEM_DARKENING_BOOST and STEM_DARKENING_LUT are defined identically in both cache.rs (lines 19-46) and rasterize.rs (lines 232-245). This is a pre-existing DRY violation not introduced by this feature, but worth noting — a single source-of-truth for these constants would prevent divergence.

DESIGN.md still references 'LCD subpixel rendering (per-channel RGB coverage, 6× horizontal oversampling)' in the fonts library description. This is now stale documentation after the grayscale switch. Not part of this feature's scope but a documentation drift risk.
README.md describes fonts/ as 'TrueType rasterizer, subpixel rendering, glyph cache'. 'subpixel rendering' is no longer accurate. Same documentation drift concern.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The .factory/library/font-rendering.md file states 'GlyphCache is ~1.3 MiB' in the 'Testing Pitfall: GlyphCache Heap Allocation' section. After this feature, GlyphCache is ~238 KiB (GLYPH_BUF_SIZE changed from W*6*H to W*H). The cache no longer overflows the default 2 MiB thread stack, so the heap-allocation pitfall may no longer be critical (though still good practice).
sharedStateObservations.evidence: cache.rs:76 — GLYPH_BUF_SIZE = GLYPH_MAX_W * GLYPH_MAX_H = 50*50 = 2500. 95 * 2500 = 237,500 bytes ≈ 232 KiB. .factory/library/font-rendering.md still says ~1.3 MiB.

sharedStateObservations.observation: system/DESIGN.md line 147 and system/README.md line 113 still describe the fonts library as using 'LCD subpixel rendering' and '6× horizontal oversampling'. These are now stale after the grayscale switch and should be updated to reflect 'grayscale anti-aliasing with vertical oversampling'.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: DESIGN.md:147 — 'LCD subpixel rendering (per-channel RGB coverage, 6× horizontal oversampling)'. README.md:113 — 'subpixel rendering'. Both contradict the actual implementation after commit 0d6f1ad.

sharedStateObservations.observation: STEM_DARKENING_BOOST and STEM_DARKENING_LUT are defined identically in both rasterize.rs and cache.rs. This duplication predates this feature but is a DRY violation that could lead to divergence if only one copy is updated in the future.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: rasterize.rs:232-245 and cache.rs:19-46 — identical constants (BOOST=90, same LUT formula). Tests import from cache.rs (drawing.rs:2519). Rasterizer uses its own copy internally.

summary: Feature passes review. The subpixel-to-grayscale migration is clean, complete, and well-tested. All four validation assertions (VAL-SUB-01 through VAL-SUB-04) are satisfied. The implementation correctly removes OVERSAMPLE_X, FIR filter, and per-channel RGB coverage from the entire pipeline (rasterizer, GlyphCache, LruGlyphCache, draw_coverage). Vertical oversampling and stem darkening are preserved. 1,782 tests pass with comprehensive coverage of the new format. QEMU visual verification confirms readable text with no color fringing. Three non-blocking observations noted: stale documentation in DESIGN.md/README.md/.factory/library/font-rendering.md, and a pre-existing DRY violation for stem darkening constants.
